title: Sum of Subarray Minimums
description: Find sum of minimum values of all subarrays using monotonic stack

examples.explanation: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Subarrays and their mins: [3] min=3, sum=3; [3,1] min=1, sum=4; [3,1,2] min=1, sum=5; [3,1,2,4] min=1, sum=6; [1] min=1, sum=7; [1,2] min=1, sum=8; [1,2,4] min=1, sum=9; [2] min=2, sum=11; [2,4] min=2, sum=13; [4] min=4, sum=17. Total sum = 17.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n³)
approaches.summary: Generate all subarrays by iterating over all start and end indices, then find the minimum in each subarray by scanning it. This is straightforward but inefficient for large arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Subarray Minimums with Brute Force O(n³)
ladders.desc: Compute the sum of minimum values across all subarrays by generating all subarrays and scanning each to find its minimum.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires finding the sum of minimum values for every possible contiguous subarray of a given array. The brute force approach is conceptually simple but computationally expensive. The intuition is that we can systematically enumerate all subarrays by varying start and end indices, then for each subarray, scan through its elements to identify the minimum value. This method works because it exhaustively checks every subarray, ensuring no minimum is missed. However, it's inefficient due to triple nesting of loops: two loops to define subarray boundaries and an inner loop to find the minimum. The algorithm involves initializing a total sum to 0, then iterating over all possible start indices from 0 to n-1. For each start, iterate over all possible end indices from start to n-1 to define a subarray. Within this, initialize a variable to track the current subarray's minimum, scan from start to end to find the minimum, add it to the total sum, and continue. Time complexity is O(n³) because for each of O(n²) subarrays, we perform an O(n) scan. Space complexity is O(1) as we only use a few variables. This connects to the parent problem by providing a baseline solution that, while correct, highlights the need for optimization (like monotonic stacks) in real-world scenarios. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, incorrectly resetting the minimum for each subarray, or missing edge cases like empty arrays (which should return 0). Real code pattern: def sumSubarrayMins(arr): total = 0; n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): min_val = arr[i]; for k in range(i, j+1): min_val = min(min_val, arr[k]); total += min_val; return total.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Subarrays and their mins: [3] min=3, sum=3; [3,1] min=1, sum=4; [3,1,2] min=1, sum=5; [3,1,2,4] min=1, sum=6; [1] min=1, sum=7; [1,2] min=1, sum=8; [1,2,4] min=1, sum=9; [2] min=2, sum=11; [2,4] min=2, sum=13; [4] min=4, sum=17. Total sum = 17.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Find Minimum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Given a subarray defined by start and end indices, scan through it to find the minimum value.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core operation within the brute force solution, responsible for identifying the minimum element in a contiguous segment of an array. The intuition is straightforward: by iterating through all elements in the subarray and keeping track of the smallest value encountered, we can reliably find the minimum. This works because comparison is transitive, and scanning ensures we check every element. The algorithm involves initializing a variable min_val with the first element of the subarray (at index start). Then, loop from start+1 to end (inclusive), updating min_val whenever a smaller element is found. Finally, return min_val. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the subarray, and in the worst case, this contributes to the O(n³) overall time. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the parent problem by being called for each subarray to compute its minimum, which is then summed. Common mistakes include starting the loop from start instead of start+1 (redundant comparison), not handling empty subarrays (should return a sentinel like infinity, but in context, subarrays are non-empty), or incorrectly updating min_val. Real code pattern: def findMinInSubarray(arr, start, end): min_val = arr[start]; for i in range(start+1, end+1): if arr[i] < min_val: min_val = arr[i]; return min_val.

examples.trace: Subarray indices 0 to 2: elements [3,1,2]. Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 3. i=1: arr[1]=1 < 3, update min_val=1. i=2: arr[2]=2 > 1, no update. Return min_val=1.
Subarray indices 1 to 3: elements [10,15,20]. Initialize min_val = arr[1] = 10. i=2: arr[2]=15 > 10, no update. i=3: arr[3]=20 > 10, no update. Return min_val=10.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generate All Subarrays by Iterating Start and End Indices
ladders.desc: Systematically enumerate all contiguous subarrays of an array using nested loops for start and end indices.
ladders.explanation: This core logic forms the backbone of the brute force approach, focusing on how to generate every possible subarray without duplication. The intuition is that any subarray can be uniquely defined by its start index i and end index j, where i ≤ j. By iterating i from 0 to n-1 and for each i, iterating j from i to n-1, we cover all combinations, ensuring each subarray is considered exactly once. This works because the array indices are discrete and ordered, allowing us to use loops to span all valid ranges. The algorithm involves initializing an empty list or counter for subarrays, then using two nested loops: outer loop for i in range(n), inner loop for j in range(i, n). For each pair (i,j), the subarray is arr[i:j+1]. Time complexity is O(n²) as there are n*(n+1)/2 subarrays. Space complexity is O(1) if just counting, or O(n²) if storing all subarrays. This connects to the level above by providing the subarrays for which we need to find minima. Common mistakes include using j < n instead of j ≤ n (missing single-element subarrays), or starting j from 0 (generating non-contiguous or duplicate subarrays). Real code pattern: def generateAllSubarrays(arr): n = len(arr); subarrays = []; for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): subarrays.append(arr[i:j+1]); return subarrays.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterate Over Array Indices with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Use nested loops to traverse all pairs of indices (i,j) where i ≤ j in an array.
ladders.explanation: This building block is essential for generating subarrays, focusing on the loop structure that defines start and end indices. The intuition is that by controlling loop variables, we can systematically cover all ordered pairs without repetition. This works because loops increment sequentially, ensuring we consider each possible start index and, for each, all valid end indices that come after or at the same position. The algorithm involves an outer loop with variable i from 0 to n-1, and an inner loop with variable j from i to n-1. For each iteration, we have a pair (i,j) representing a subarray from i to j inclusive. Time complexity is O(n²) due to the nested loops. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being the mechanism to enumerate subarrays. Common mistakes include using j from 0 to n-1 (which would generate pairs where i > j, invalid for subarrays), or not including j = i (missing single-element subarrays). Real code pattern: n = len(arr); for i in range(n): for j in range(i, n): # process subarray arr[i:j+1].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Find Minimum in a List by Scanning
ladders.desc: Scan through a list of numbers to find the smallest value using a loop and comparison.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation is fundamental to many algorithms, including our brute force solution. The intuition is that by examining each element one by one and keeping track of the smallest seen so far, we can determine the overall minimum. This works because the minimum is defined relative to all elements, and scanning ensures we consider each. The algorithm involves initializing a variable min_val with the first element of the list (if the list is non-empty). Then, iterate through the remaining elements, comparing each to min_val, and update min_val if a smaller element is found. Finally, return min_val. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the list length, as we check each element once. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the level above by being used within subarrays to find their minima. Common mistakes include not handling empty lists (should raise an error or return a sentinel), initializing min_val incorrectly (e.g., to 0, which might not be in the list), or skipping the first element in the loop. Real code pattern: def findMin(lst): if not lst: return None; min_val = lst[0]; for num in lst[1:]: if num < min_val: min_val = num; return min_val.

examples.trace: List: [5,2,8,1,9]. Initialize min_val = 5. Iterate: num=2 < 5, update min_val=2; num=8 > 2, no update; num=1 < 2, update min_val=1; num=9 > 1, no update. Return min_val=1.
List: [10,20,30]. Initialize min_val = 10. Iterate: num=20 > 10, no update; num=30 > 10, no update. Return min_val=10.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understand Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Learn what an array is, how to access elements by index, and basic iteration with loops.
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept is prerequisite for all higher levels, introducing arrays as a fundamental data structure. An array is an ordered collection of elements, each accessible by a numerical index starting from 0. The intuition is that arrays allow efficient storage and retrieval of data based on position. This works because memory is allocated contiguously, enabling O(1) access time. Key operations include: declaring an array (e.g., arr = [3,1,2]), accessing an element (e.g., arr[0] gives 3), and iterating using a loop (e.g., for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i])). Time complexity for access is O(1), for iteration is O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to the level above by providing the basic structure on which we perform operations like finding minima. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., accessing arr[n] for an array of size n, which is out of bounds), confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, or not initializing arrays properly. Real code pattern: arr = [10,20,30]; print(arr[0]) # outputs 10; for i in range(len(arr)): print(arr[i]) # prints 10,20,30.

examples.trace: Array: [5,10,15]. Indices: 0->5, 1->10, 2->15. Access arr[1] returns element at index 1, which is 10.
Array: [2,4,6,8]. Loop i from 0 to 3: i=0 print arr[0]=2; i=1 print arr[1]=4; i=2 print arr[2]=6; i=3 print arr[3]=8.

approaches.name: Optimized Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: Iterate over all subarrays by fixing a start index and expanding the end, maintaining the running minimum as the subarray grows. This avoids redundant scans within subarrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Subarray Minimums using Optimized Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete O(n²) solution that iterates through all subarrays while efficiently tracking minimums.
ladders.explanation:
This approach solves the problem by generating all possible subarrays while avoiding redundant minimum calculations. The intuition is that when we fix a starting index and expand the subarray to the right, we can maintain the current minimum value as we add new elements, rather than scanning the entire subarray each time. This reduces the naive O(n³) approach to O(n²).

Algorithm:
1. Initialize total_sum = 0
2. For each starting index i from 0 to n-1:
   - Set current_min = arr[i]
   - For each ending index j from i to n-1:
     - Update current_min = min(current_min, arr[j])
     - Add current_min to total_sum
3. Return total_sum

Time Complexity: O(n²) - We have nested loops where the inner loop runs n-i times for each i, resulting in n(n+1)/2 iterations.
Space Complexity: O(1) - We only use a few variables.

Connection to L1: This full solution builds upon the key subroutine of maintaining a running minimum while expanding subarrays.

Common mistakes:
1. Forgetting to reset current_min for each new starting index
2. Using O(n³) approach by scanning each subarray from scratch
3. Integer overflow with large sums (use modulo if specified)

Real code pattern:
def sumSubarrayMins(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        current_min = arr[i]
        for j in range(i, n):
            current_min = min(current_min, arr[j])
            total += current_min
    return total

examples.trace:
Starting with i=0 (value 3):
- j=0: subarray [3], min=3, sum=3
- j=1: subarray [3,1], min=min(3,1)=1, sum=3+1=4
- j=2: subarray [3,1,2], min=min(1,2)=1, sum=4+1=5
- j=3: subarray [3,1,2,4], min=min(1,4)=1, sum=5+1=6
Total after i=0: 6

i=1 (value 1):
- j=1: subarray [1], min=1, sum=6+1=7
- j=2: subarray [1,2], min=min(1,2)=1, sum=7+1=8
- j=3: subarray [1,2,4], min=min(1,4)=1, sum=8+1=9
Total after i=1: 9

i=2 (value 2):
- j=2: subarray [2], min=2, sum=9+2=11
- j=3: subarray [2,4], min=min(2,4)=2, sum=11+2=13
Total after i=2: 13

i=3 (value 4):
- j=3: subarray [4], min=4, sum=13+4=17
Final total: 17

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Maintaining Running Minimum While Expanding Subarrays
ladders.desc: The core technique of updating minimum efficiently as subarray expands.
ladders.explanation:
This subroutine demonstrates how to efficiently track the minimum value as we expand a subarray to the right. The intuition is that when we add a new element to an existing subarray, we only need to compare it with the current minimum - we don't need to scan the entire subarray again.

Algorithm:
1. Start with a fixed beginning index i
2. Initialize current_min = arr[i]
3. For j from i to n-1:
   - Update current_min = min(current_min, arr[j])
   - current_min now represents the minimum of subarray arr[i..j]

Time Complexity: O(n) for processing all subarrays starting at a fixed i
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to L0: This is the inner loop logic that makes the O(n²) solution efficient compared to naive O(n³).

Common mistakes:
1. Not initializing current_min properly for each starting index
2. Comparing with wrong element (should compare with arr[j], not arr[i])
3. Forgetting that current_min can only decrease or stay the same as we expand

Real code pattern:
def process_subarrays_from_index(arr, i):
    current_min = arr[i]
    results = []
    for j in range(i, len(arr)):
        current_min = min(current_min, arr[j])
        results.append(current_min)
    return results

examples.trace:
Starting with i=1, arr[1]=1
Initialize current_min = 1

j=1: subarray [1], current_min=1
j=2: compare current_min(1) with arr[2]=2 → min=1
j=3: compare current_min(1) with arr[3]=4 → min=1

Resulting minimums: [1,1,1]

Starting with i=0, arr[0]=5
Initialize current_min = 5

j=0: subarray [5], current_min=5
j=1: compare 5 with arr[1]=4 → min=4
j=2: compare 4 with arr[2]=3 → min=3
j=3: compare 3 with arr[3]=2 → min=2
j=4: compare 2 with arr[4]=1 → min=1

Result: [5,4,3,2,1]

ladders.title: Core Logic — Generating All Subarrays with Nested Loops
ladders.desc: Systematically creating all possible contiguous subarrays of an array.
ladders.explanation:
This level focuses on generating all subarrays using nested loops. The intuition is that every subarray can be uniquely identified by its starting and ending indices. By fixing a start index and varying the end index, we can generate all subarrays in a systematic way.

Algorithm:
1. For start index i from 0 to n-1:
2. For end index j from i to n-1:
3. Subarray = arr[i..j]

Time Complexity: O(n²) - There are n(n+1)/2 subarrays
Space Complexity: O(1) for generation (not counting storage)

Connection to L1: This provides the framework within which we apply the running minimum technique.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting inner loop at 0 instead of i (creates duplicates)
2. Using i <= j instead of i to n-1 (misses single-element subarrays)
3. Not understanding there are n(n+1)/2 total subarrays

Real code pattern:
def generate_all_subarrays(arr):
    n = len(arr)
    subarrays = []
    for i in range(n):
        for j in range(i, n):
            subarrays.append(arr[i:j+1])
    return subarrays

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Minimum in a Subarray
ladders.desc: Computing the minimum value within a given contiguous subarray.
ladders.explanation:
This fundamental operation finds the smallest element in a specified subarray. The intuition is simple: we need to examine each element in the range and track the smallest value encountered.

Algorithm:
1. Initialize min_val = arr[start]
2. For index k from start to end:
3. If arr[k] < min_val, update min_val = arr[k]
4. Return min_val

Time Complexity: O(k) where k = end-start+1 (size of subarray)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to L2: This is the operation we need to perform for each generated subarray in the naive approach.

Common mistakes:
1. Starting comparison with 0 instead of first element
2. Using <= instead of < (both work but < is standard)
3. Not handling empty subarrays (shouldn't occur in this problem)

Real code pattern:
def find_min_in_subarray(arr, start, end):
    min_val = arr[start]
    for i in range(start, end+1):
        if arr[i] < min_val:
            min_val = arr[i]
    return min_val

examples.trace:
Subarray: [3,1,4]
Initialize min_val = arr[0] = 3

Check arr[1] = 1: 1 < 3 → min_val = 1
Check arr[2] = 4: 4 < 1? No → min_val stays 1

Final min_val = 1

Subarray: [2,8,3,1]
Initialize min_val = arr[1] = 2

Check arr[2] = 8: 8 < 2? No
Check arr[3] = 3: 3 < 2? No
Check arr[4] = 1: 1 < 2 → min_val = 1

Final min_val = 1

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Two Numbers
ladders.desc: Finding the smaller of two values using comparison.
ladders.explanation:
This is the most fundamental operation: comparing two numbers to determine which is smaller. The intuition is straightforward - we need to check if one number is less than another.

Algorithm:
1. Given two numbers a and b
2. If a < b, return a
3. Else, return b

Time Complexity: O(1)
Space Complexity: O(1)

Connection to L3: This comparison is repeated within the loop to find the minimum in a subarray.

Common mistakes:
1. Using wrong comparison operator (>, >= instead of <)
2. Not considering equal values (a < b handles equality correctly)
3. Forgetting to return a value

Real code pattern:
def min_of_two(a, b):
    if a < b:
        return a
    else:
        return b

# Or simply: return a if a < b else b

examples.trace:
Compare 3 and 5
3 < 5 is true
Therefore return 3

Compare 7 and 2
7 < 2 is false
Therefore return 2

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Basic array operations: accessing elements and iterating with indices.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational level covers the basic concepts of arrays: they are ordered collections of elements where each element can be accessed by its position (index). The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values and work with them systematically using indices.

Key concepts:
1. Arrays are zero-indexed (first element at index 0)
2. Elements are accessed using arr[index]
3. We can iterate through arrays using loops

Basic operations:
1. Access: arr[i] gets element at position i
2. Length: len(arr) gives number of elements
3. Iteration: for i in range(len(arr)): process arr[i]

Connection to L4: Array access provides the values we need to compare.

Common beginner mistakes:
1. Using 1-based indexing (thinking arr[1] is first element)
2. Accessing out of bounds (arr[len(arr)] is invalid)
3. Confusing index with value

Real code pattern:
# Creating an array
arr = [3, 1, 4, 2]

# Accessing elements
first = arr[0]  # 3
second = arr[1] # 1

# Iterating
for i in range(len(arr)):
    print(f"Index {i}: Value {arr[i]}")

examples.trace:
Array has 4 elements
arr[0] = 10
arr[1] = 20
arr[2] = 30
arr[3] = 40

Iteration:
i=0 → arr[0]=10
i=1 → arr[1]=20
i=2 → arr[2]=30
i=3 → arr[3]=40

Array length = 5
First element: arr[0] = 5
Third element: arr[2] = 8
Last element: arr[4] = 9

Trying to access arr[5] would cause error (out of bounds)

approaches.name: Monotonic Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a monotonic increasing stack to find, for each element, the number of subarrays where it is the minimum by determining its left and right boundaries. This leverages stack properties to compute contributions efficiently.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Sum of Subarray Minimums with Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Complete O(n) solution using monotonic stack to compute sum of minimums of all subarrays.
ladders.explanation: The problem requires finding the sum of minimum elements across all possible contiguous subarrays of an array. A naive approach would be O(n³) by generating all subarrays and finding their minimums. The efficient O(n) solution leverages a monotonic increasing stack to determine, for each element arr[i], the number of subarrays where it serves as the minimum. This is achieved by finding the left boundary (previous smaller element) and right boundary (next smaller element) for each element. The contribution of arr[i] to the total sum is arr[i] * count_of_subarrays_where_it_is_minimum. The count is computed as (i - left_boundary) * (right_boundary - i), where left_boundary is the index of the previous smaller element (or -1 if none) and right_boundary is the index of the next smaller element (or n if none). We use a monotonic increasing stack to efficiently find these boundaries in a single pass. The stack stores indices, and we process each element, popping from the stack while the current element is smaller than the stack's top element, which helps determine the right boundary for the popped element. Simultaneously, the new top after popping gives the left boundary. We handle elements with equal values carefully to avoid double-counting by using strict inequalities. The total sum is accumulated modulo 1e9+7 as per typical constraints. The intuition is that each element's contribution is proportional to its value and the number of subarrays it dominates as the minimum, which is determined by how far it can extend left and right without encountering a smaller element. This approach transforms a combinatorial problem into a linear scan with stack operations, making it efficient and elegant.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Initialize stack=[], total=0. Process i=0, val=3: stack empty, push 0. i=1, val=1: while stack not empty and arr[stack[-1]]=3 > 1: pop index 0, left boundary = -1 (stack empty), right boundary = 1, contribution = 3 * (0 - (-1)) * (1 - 0) = 3 * 1 * 1 = 3, total=3. Push i=1. i=2, val=2: stack top index 1 has val=1 <= 2, push 2. i=3, val=4: stack top index 2 has val=2 <= 4, push 3. After loop, process remaining stack: pop index 3, left=2, right=4, contribution=4*(3-2)*(4-3)=4*1*1=4, total=7. pop index 2, left=1, right=4, contribution=2*(2-1)*(4-2)=2*1*2=4, total=11. pop index 1, left=-1, right=4, contribution=1*(1-(-1))*(4-1)=1*2*3=6, total=17. Output 17.

Array: [11,81,94,43,3], n=5. Initialize stack=[], total=0. i=0, val=11: push 0. i=1, val=81: push 1. i=2, val=94: push 2. i=3, val=43: while stack top index 2 has val=94 > 43: pop 2, left=1, right=3, contribution=94*(2-1)*(3-2)=94*1*1=94, total=94. Now stack top index 1 has val=81 > 43: pop 1, left=0, right=3, contribution=81*(1-0)*(3-1)=81*1*2=162, total=256. Push i=3. i=4, val=3: while stack top index 3 has val=43 > 3: pop 3, left=0, right=4, contribution=43*(3-0)*(4-3)=43*3*1=129, total=385. Now stack top index 0 has val=11 > 3: pop 0, left=-1, right=4, contribution=11*(0-(-1))*(4-0)=11*1*4=44, total=429. Push i=4. Process remaining: pop index 4, left=-1, right=5, contribution=3*(4-(-1))*(5-4)=3*5*1=15, total=444. Output 444.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Boundaries with Monotonic Stack
ladders.desc: Compute left and right boundaries for each element using a monotonic increasing stack.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the full solution. For each element in the array, we need to find two boundaries: the left boundary (index of the previous smaller element) and the right boundary (index of the next smaller element). These boundaries define the range where the current element is the minimum. We use a monotonic increasing stack (storing indices) to achieve this in O(n) time. The algorithm processes the array from left to right. For each index i, while the stack is not empty and the current element arr[i] is less than the element at the stack's top index, we pop the top index j. For this popped element, its right boundary is i (since arr[i] is smaller), and its left boundary is the new top of the stack (or -1 if stack becomes empty). We then compute the contribution of arr[j] as described. After processing all pops, we push i onto the stack. After the loop, any remaining indices in the stack have no smaller element to the right, so their right boundary is n (array length). We process them similarly. The intuition is that the stack maintains indices of elements in increasing order of their values. When we encounter a smaller element, it serves as the right boundary for all larger elements in the stack, allowing us to compute their contributions efficiently. This avoids nested loops and leverages the stack's LIFO property to handle boundaries correctly. Common mistakes include not handling equal values properly (use <= or < consistently to avoid double-counting), forgetting to process remaining stack elements, and off-by-one errors in boundary calculations. The time complexity is O(n) as each element is pushed and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Initialize stack=[], boundaries=[]. i=0, val=3: stack empty, push 0. i=1, val=1: while stack not empty and arr[stack[-1]]=3 > 1: pop j=0, left=-1 (stack empty), right=1, add (0,-1,1). Push i=1. i=2, val=2: stack top index 1 has val=1 <= 2, push 2. i=3, val=4: stack top index 2 has val=2 <= 4, push 3. After loop, process stack: pop j=3, left=2, right=4, add (3,2,4). pop j=2, left=1, right=4, add (2,1,4). pop j=1, left=-1, right=4, add (1,-1,4). Output list of (index, left, right).

Array: [5,4,3,2,1], n=5. Initialize stack=[], boundaries=[]. i=0, val=5: push 0. i=1, val=4: while stack top index 0 has val=5 > 4: pop j=0, left=-1, right=1, add (0,-1,1). Push i=1. i=2, val=3: while stack top index 1 has val=4 > 3: pop j=1, left=-1, right=2, add (1,-1,2). Push i=2. i=3, val=2: while stack top index 2 has val=3 > 2: pop j=2, left=-1, right=3, add (2,-1,3). Push i=3. i=4, val=1: while stack top index 3 has val=2 > 1: pop j=3, left=-1, right=4, add (3,-1,4). Push i=4. Process stack: pop j=4, left=-1, right=5, add (4,-1,5). Output boundaries.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Contribution of Each Element
ladders.desc: Calculate how many subarrays have a given element as minimum using boundaries.
ladders.explanation: Once we have the left and right boundaries for an element at index i, we can compute the number of subarrays where this element is the minimum. The left boundary L is the index of the previous smaller element (or -1 if none), and the right boundary R is the index of the next smaller element (or n if none). The element arr[i] is the minimum in any subarray that starts between L+1 and i (inclusive) and ends between i and R-1 (inclusive). The number of possible start indices is (i - L), and the number of possible end indices is (R - i). Therefore, the total number of subarrays where arr[i] is the minimum is (i - L) * (R - i). The contribution of arr[i] to the total sum is arr[i] multiplied by this count. For example, if arr[i]=5, L=1, i=3, R=6, then start indices can be 2 or 3 (2 choices), end indices can be 3,4,5 (3 choices), so 2*3=6 subarrays have 5 as minimum, contributing 5*6=30 to the sum. This logic relies on the fact that within the range (L,R), arr[i] is strictly smaller than all other elements (by definition of boundaries), so it will be the minimum in any subarray fully contained within that range that includes i. If there are equal elements, we must define boundaries carefully to avoid double-counting; typically, we use strict inequality for one side and non-strict for the other. The intuition is combinatorial: each subarray is defined by a start and end index, and the boundaries restrict which pairs result in arr[i] being the minimum. This transforms the problem from checking all subarrays to a simple multiplication per element, enabling O(n) efficiency when boundaries are found efficiently.

examples.trace: Given arr[0]=3, L=-1, i=0, R=1. Number of start indices = i - L = 0 - (-1) = 1 (only start at 0). Number of end indices = R - i = 1 - 0 = 1 (only end at 0). So count = 1*1 = 1 subarray: [3]. Contribution = 3 * 1 = 3.

Given arr[1]=1, L=-1, i=1, R=4. Start indices: i - L = 1 - (-1) = 2 (start at 0 or 1). End indices: R - i = 4 - 1 = 3 (end at 1,2, or 3). Count = 2*3 = 6 subarrays: [1], [1,2], [1,2,4], [3,1], [3,1,2], [3,1,2,4]. Contribution = 1 * 6 = 6.

ladders.title: Building Block — Monotonic Stack Operations
ladders.desc: Implement a monotonic increasing stack to maintain indices of elements in sorted order.
ladders.explanation: A monotonic stack is a stack where elements are maintained in either increasing or decreasing order. For this problem, we use a monotonic increasing stack (non-decreasing) to store indices of array elements. The key operations are push and pop, with conditions based on the current element's value. As we iterate through the array, for each index i, we compare arr[i] with the element at the stack's top index. If arr[i] is less than the top element (for strict increasing) or less than or equal (for non-decreasing), we pop from the stack until the condition is false or the stack is empty. This popping process helps identify boundaries: when we pop an index j, the current index i becomes its right boundary, and the new stack top (if any) becomes its left boundary. After popping, we push i onto the stack. The stack thus always contains indices in increasing order of their corresponding values, which allows efficient boundary detection. The intuition is that the stack acts as a memory of previous elements that haven't yet found a smaller element to their right. When a smaller element arrives, it resolves these pending elements. This pattern is common in problems involving next/previous smaller/greater elements. Time complexity is O(n) because each index is pushed once and popped at most once. Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case. Common mistakes include incorrect comparison operators (leading to infinite loops or wrong boundaries), not handling empty stack cases, and forgetting to push the current index after popping. Real code pattern: initialize an empty stack, loop through indices, while stack not empty and arr[stack[-1]] > arr[i], pop and process, then push i.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4]. Start stack=[]. i=0, val=3: stack empty, push 0 → stack=[0]. i=1, val=1: while stack not empty and arr[stack[-1]]=3 > 1: pop 0 → stack=[]. Push 1 → stack=[1]. i=2, val=2: stack top index 1 has val=1 <= 2, push 2 → stack=[1,2]. i=3, val=4: stack top index 2 has val=2 <= 4, push 3 → stack=[1,2,3]. Final stack=[1,2,3].

Array: [5,4,3,2,1]. Start stack=[]. i=0, val=5: push 0 → [0]. i=1, val=4: while stack top index 0 has val=5 > 4: pop 0 → []. Push 1 → [1]. i=2, val=3: while stack top index 1 has val=4 > 3: pop 1 → []. Push 2 → [2]. i=3, val=2: while stack top index 2 has val=3 > 2: pop 2 → []. Push 3 → [3]. i=4, val=1: while stack top index 3 has val=2 > 1: pop 3 → []. Push 4 → [4]. Final stack=[4].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Finding Next Smaller Element
ladders.desc: For each element, find the index of the next element that is smaller.
ladders.explanation: This operation is a fundamental step in the monotonic stack approach. Given an array, for each element at index i, we want to find the index j > i such that arr[j] is the first element smaller than arr[i]. If no such element exists, we define j as n (the length of the array). This is known as the 'next smaller element' problem. A naive approach would use nested loops for O(n²) time, but we can use a monotonic increasing stack for O(n) time. The algorithm: initialize an empty stack and an array next_smaller of size n initialized to n. Iterate through indices from 0 to n-1. For each index i, while the stack is not empty and arr[i] is less than the element at the stack's top index, pop the top index j and set next_smaller[j] = i. Then push i onto the stack. After the loop, any indices remaining in the stack have next_smaller = n. The intuition is that the stack keeps indices of elements for which we haven't yet found a smaller element to the right. When we encounter a smaller element, it becomes the next smaller for all larger elements in the stack, which we pop and assign. This operation is crucial because it helps determine the right boundary for each element in the full problem. Time complexity is O(n) as each index is pushed and popped once. Space complexity is O(n) for the stack and result array. Common mistakes include using wrong comparison (e.g., <= instead of < for strict smaller), not initializing next_smaller properly, and off-by-one in indices.

examples.trace: Array: [3,1,2,4], n=4. Initialize stack=[], next=[4,4,4,4]. i=0, val=3: stack empty, push 0. i=1, val=1: while stack not empty and arr[stack[-1]]=3 > 1: pop j=0, set next[0]=1. Push i=1. i=2, val=2: stack top index 1 has val=1 <= 2, push 2. i=3, val=4: stack top index 2 has val=2 <= 4, push 3. After loop, stack=[1,2,3], their next values remain 4. Output next=[1,4,4,4].

Array: [5,4,3,2,1], n=5. Initialize stack=[], next=[5,5,5,5,5]. i=0, val=5: push 0. i=1, val=4: while stack top index 0 has val=5 > 4: pop j=0, set next[0]=1. Push i=1. i=2, val=3: while stack top index 1 has val=4 > 3: pop j=1, set next[1]=2. Push i=2. i=3, val=2: while stack top index 2 has val=3 > 2: pop j=2, set next[2]=3. Push i=3. i=4, val=1: while stack top index 3 has val=2 > 1: pop j=3, set next[3]=4. Push i=4. After loop, stack=[4], next[4]=5. Output next=[1,2,3,4,5].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Stack Data Structure and LIFO
ladders.desc: Understand stack operations (push, pop) and Last-In-First-Out principle.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a fundamental data structure that follows the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. It supports two main operations: push (add an element to the top) and pop (remove the top element). Additional operations include peek (view the top element without removing) and isEmpty (check if the stack is empty). In programming, stacks can be implemented using arrays or linked lists. The LIFO property means the most recently added element is the first one to be removed. This is analogous to a stack of plates: you add a plate to the top and remove from the top. In the context of the monotonic stack approach, we use a stack to store indices of array elements. The push operation adds the current index, and the pop operation removes indices when a smaller element is encountered. The stack helps maintain an order that allows efficient retrieval of previous elements. For example, when finding the next smaller element, the stack keeps track of elements for which we haven't found a smaller one yet. When a smaller element arrives, we pop from the stack to resolve these. This leverages LIFO to process elements in reverse order of their addition. Understanding stacks is crucial because they enable O(1) time for push and pop, making algorithms efficient. Common mistakes include not checking if the stack is empty before popping or peeking, which can cause errors. Real code pattern: use a list as a stack in Python, with append for push and pop for pop, and check len(stack) > 0 for emptiness.

examples.input: Push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop, push 4
examples.trace: Start stack=[]. Push 1: stack=[1]. Push 2: stack=[1,2]. Push 3: stack=[1,2,3]. Pop: remove 3 → stack=[1,2]. Pop: remove 2 → stack=[1]. Push 4: stack=[1,4]. Final stack=[1,4].

examples.input: Push 5, pop, push 6, push 7, pop, pop
examples.trace: Start stack=[]. Push 5: stack=[5]. Pop: remove 5 → stack=[]. Push 6: stack=[6]. Push 7: stack=[6,7]. Pop: remove 7 → stack=[6]. Pop: remove 6 → stack=[]. Final stack=[].

testCases.input: Push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop, push 4
Push 5, pop, push 6, push 7, pop, pop
Push a, push b, push c, pop, push d
Push x, pop, push y, pop
Push 1, push 2, push 3, push 4, pop, pop
Push z, pop, push w, push v, pop, pop
